behaviors.description: Log entries with the same trace ID are grouped together regardless of which microservice emitted them.
behaviors.justification: Trace ID grouping is a deterministic algorithm that operates on in-memory structured data. The failure boundary is the matching/grouping logic itself, not the source of log entries. A Unit test with mocked log entries from multiple simulated microservices (different service names in each entry) will catch failures in trace ID extraction, comparison, or group formation. No real I/O or external services are needed—the test boundary is the grouping algorithm.
behaviors.test_description: Create mocked log entries with the same trace ID but different service origins (e.g., service-a, service-b, service-c). Pass them to the grouping function and verify all entries are collected under a single trace group, preserving their service metadata.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The test creates mocked entries with identical trace IDs and verifies the algorithm groups them correctly. This is sufficient because grouping is a pure function on structured data, not dependent on actual service ingestion.

behaviors.description: The timeline view orders entries by timestamp, correctly handling clock skew of up to 500ms between services.
behaviors.justification: Timestamp-based ordering and clock skew tolerance are deterministic algorithms. The failure boundary is the sort order and skew compensation logic, not the source of the timestamps. A Unit test with mocked entries whose timestamps span a 500ms window (some delayed, some advanced) will catch failures in ordering, skew detection, or tolerance thresholds. Real clock synchronization between services is not needed—test data can encode clock differences directly.
behaviors.test_description: Create mocked log entries with timestamps differing by 0–500ms (simulating clock skew across services). Verify the timeline view orders them by timestamp and that entries within the skew window are handled as contemporaneous (not reordered incorrectly).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The test provides specific timestamp values to test ordering and skew handling. This is sufficient because both are deterministic algorithms; no real time source or NTP interaction is required.

behaviors.description: A latency spike (p99 above 3x the baseline) is detected and flagged in the anomaly report.
behaviors.justification: Spike detection is a deterministic statistical computation: compute baseline percentile (p50 or p99), compute current p99, check if current > 3x baseline, flag if true. The failure boundary is the percentile logic and comparison, not the source of latency data. A Unit test with mocked latency values (baseline set, then spike injected) will catch failures in percentile calculation, threshold comparison, or flag generation. No real request tracing or external metrics are needed.
behaviors.test_description: Create a mocked baseline of latencies (e.g., p50=100ms, p99=200ms from 100 samples) and a spike window with p99=700ms (>3x baseline). Verify the spike is detected and the anomaly flag is set in the output report.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The test provides mocked latency values and verifies the spike detection algorithm. This is sufficient because spike detection is pure statistics on numeric inputs; no real latency measurement is required.

behaviors.description: Malformed JSON log entries are skipped without crashing the pipeline, and the count of skipped entries is reported.
behaviors.justification: Error handling and counting must work correctly under real pipeline conditions. A Unit test with mocked malformed entries could catch basic parsing failures, but real failures often emerge from integration: concurrent processing might introduce race conditions in counting, file streaming might cause buffering issues that lose entries during skip, or the parser might fail differently on truncated/partial input from real I/O. An Integration test with real file I/O or a simulated stream will exercise error handling under realistic conditions and verify both the pipeline stability and accurate count reporting.
behaviors.test_description: Ingest a real file or simulated stream containing a mix of well-formed and malformed JSON entries (e.g., truncated objects, invalid UTF-8, missing quotes). Verify the pipeline completes without crashing, count the successfully parsed entries, and assert the skipped count matches the malformed entries in the input.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The test uses real file I/O (or a realistic streaming source) to exercise the full error-handling path. This catches buffering, concurrency, and I/O-related failures that a pure Unit test with mocked entries would miss.

behaviors.description: The tool handles a burst of 10,000 log entries per second without dropping entries or falling behind.
behaviors.justification: Performance under sustained load depends on OS-level behavior: process scheduling, memory allocation, I/O buffering, and resource contention. A Unit test with an in-memory mocked stream cannot replicate these. An Integration test with real file I/O might catch some bottlenecks (e.g., slow disk writes), but a System test that feeds 10k entries/sec via real I/O for an extended period is necessary to observe OS-level resource pressure, GC pauses, scheduler latency, and buffer overflow behavior. Only then can you verify the pipeline sustains throughput without dropping entries or falling behind.
behaviors.test_description: Feed 10,000 log entries per second to the tool via real I/O (file, database, or network stream) for 5–10 seconds. Monitor CPU, memory, and I/O metrics. Verify no entries are dropped (compare input count to processed count), confirm the pipeline does not fall behind (measure end-to-end latency), and ensure the process does not crash or exhaust resources.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The test applies real OS-level load via actual I/O and monitors system resource behavior. This is necessary because throughput failures emerge from scheduling, memory pressure, and I/O contention—invisible to mocked in-memory tests.
